```
Intel supports optimised TensorFlow on both CPU and GPU, using the [Intel
Extension for TensorFlow](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-tensorflow/latest/get_started.html).
On Dawn this version on TensorFlow is accessible as a conda environment named tensorflow-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate tensorflow-gpu
```

To run on the PVCs, there should be no need to modify your code - the Intel
optimised implementation will run automatically on the GPU, assuming it has been
installed as intel-extension-for-tensorflow[xpu].

## Jax/OpenXLA[¶](#jax-openxla "Permalink to this headline")

Documentation can be found on GitHub: [Intel OpenXLA](https://github.com/intel/intel-extension-for-openxla)

## Julia[¶](#julia "Permalink to this headline")

This is currently known not to work correctly on PVC GPUs. (Mar 2024)

# Dawn - Machine Learning & Data Science frameworks[¶](#dawn-machine-learning-data-science-frameworks "Permalink to this headline")

We provide a set of pre-populated Conda environments based on the Intel
Distribution for Python:

```
module av intelpython-conda
conda info -e
```

This module provides environments for PyTorch and Tensorflow.

Please note that Intel code and documentation sometimes refers to ‘XPUs’, a more
generic term for accelerators, GPU or otherwise. For Dawn, ‘XPU’ and ‘GPU’ can
usually be considered interchangeable.

## PyTorch[¶](#pytorch "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch on Intel GPUs is supported by the [Intel Extension for PyTorch](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-pytorch/xpu/latest/).
On Dawn this version of PyTorch is accessible as a conda environment named pytorch-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate pytorch-gpu
```

Adapting your code to run on the PVCs is straightforward and only takes a few
lines of code. For details, see the official documentation - but as a quick
example:

```
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex

...

# Enable GPU
model = model.to('xpu')
data = data.to('xpu')

model = ipex.optimize(model, dtype=torch.float32)
```

## TensorFlow[¶](#tensorflow "Permalink to this headline")

Intel supports optimised TensorFlow on both CPU and GPU, using the [Intel
Extension for TensorFlow](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-tensorflow/latest/get_started.html).
On Dawn this version on TensorFlow is accessible as a conda environment named tensorflow-gpu:

```
module load

# Running jobs on Dawn[¶](#running-jobs-on-dawn "Permalink to this headline")

## Software[¶](#software "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC nodes run [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat
Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9). The Sapphire Rapids CPUs on these
nodes are also more modern and support newer instructions than most other CSD3
partitions.

To ensure your environment is clean and set up correctly for Dawn, please purge your modules and load the
base Dawn environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
```

As we provide a separate set of modules specifically for dawn nodes, in
general, we don’t support running software built for other CSD3 partitions on
Dawn nodes. Therefore you are strongly recommended to rebuild your software on
the Dawn nodes rather than try to run binaries previously compiled on CSD3.

Be aware that the software environment for Dawn is optimised for its hardware and
the binaries may fail to run on other CSD3 nodes, including cpu (*login-p*) and
icelake (*login-q*) login nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this
new environment, we recommend requesting an interactive node with command:

```
sintr -t 01:00:00 -A YOURPROJECT-DAWN-GPU -p pvc9 -N 1 -c 24 --gres=gpu:1
```

The nodes are named according to the scheme *pvc-s-[1-256]*.

## Slurm partition[¶](#slurm-partition "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC (pvc-s) nodes are in a new **pvc9** Slurm partition.

Dawn Slurm projects follow the CSD3 naming convention for GPU projects and contain
units of GPU hours. Additionally Dawn project names

ed;
only the ‘new’ compilers (icx, icpx and ifx) are supported and are the only
ones that support GPUs.

To enable SYCL support:

```
icpx -fsycl
```

For OpenMP offload (note -fiopenmp, not -fopenmp):

```
# C
icx -fiopenmp -fopenmp-targets=spir64
# Fortran
ifx -fiopenmp -fopenmp-targets=spir64
```

Both Intel MPI and the oneMKL performance libraries support both CPU and the PVC
GPUs, and can be found as follows:

```
module av intel-oneapi-mpi
module av intel-oneapi-mkl
```

### Other recommendations[¶](#other-recommendations "Permalink to this headline")

Further useful information about running on Intel GPUs can be found in Intel’s
[oneAPI GPU Optimization Guide](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/docs/oneapi/optimization-guide-gpu/).

nstead of 8000 MiB here.

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using *Intel MPI 2021.6.0* on icelake. There are
other, related changes to the default environment seen by jobs running
on the icelake nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against the
icelake environment, the simplest option is to login directly to one
of the login-icelake nodes, i.e. to
**login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk**. Alternatively, request an
[interactive node](#recommendations-for-running-on-icelake) and work on a icelake node directly.

For reference, the default environment on the icelake (cpu-q) nodes is provided by loading a module as follows:

```
module purge
module load rhel8/default-icl
```

However since the CPU type on cpu-q is different, than on cclake, it is not recommended to build
software intended to run on cpu-q on a different flavour of node.

Hello HPC support team,

Name: Liam Collins-Jones, Account: lc984, PI: Gemma Bale
I would like to install Toast++ software (http://web4.cs.ucl.ac.uk/research/vis/toast/download_src.html ). I attempted doing this myself towards the start of 2025 but was unsuccessful. Would anyone on your end be able to install this on my account? Happy to provide more details etc.
Thanks! Liam.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Source Build / User-Space Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help installing Toast++ in their own workspace after an earlier unsuccessful attempt. This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a request for a central software install. The best initial helpdesk response is to gather any previous build errors and, if needed, attempt a clean user-space build internally before sending tested instructions.

ACTION
1) Confirm the source/version the user is trying to build.
2) Ask for any error output from the user's earlier build attempt if available.
3) If needed, attempt a clean user-space build in `hpc-work` and return tested instructions.
4) Prefer a user-space install unless there is evidence of wider demand for a central package.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Liam,

This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a central software installation.

If you still have the output from your previous build attempt, please send it to us. If needed, we can also try a clean build internally and then send you tested instructions for building Toast++ in your workspace.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:

Category: Software Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Yes
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user, Liam Collins-Jones (lc984), under PI Gemma Bale, requires assistance with installing Toast++ software. The user previously attempted installation but was unsuccessful.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Escalate the ticket to the software support team for further assistance with the installation of Toast++.
2. Inform the user that the request has been escalated and provide an estimated timeline for resolution if possible.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Liam,

Thank you for your request. We have escalated your request for the installation of Toast++ software to our software support team. They will assist you with the installation process.

Please provide any additional details or error messages you encountered during your previous attempt to install Toast++. This information will help our team to better assist you.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any software installation complies with the HPC service policies and guidelines.
- Verify the software source and installation process to ensure security and compatibility with the HPC environment.

# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a

ser would lose nothing by requesting 13000 MiB instead of 8000 MiB here.

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We recommend using *Intel MPI 2020* on Cascade Lake. There are other,
related changes to the default environment seen by jobs running on the
cclake nodes. If you wish to recompile or test in the cascade lake environment, the simplest option is to login directly to one of the login-cascadelake nodes, i.e. to login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Alternatively, request an [interactive node](#recommendations-for-running-on-cclake) and work on a cclake node directly.

# PI Dawn Access[¶](#pi-dawn-access "Permalink to this headline")

## PI Accessing Portal[¶](#pi-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (IdP of last resort)

If you encounter any issues, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance.

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.
Otherwise, please proceed to the [Identity of Last Resort](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-pi.html#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid) section.

### **University Login (MyAccessID)**

Step 1: Click the “MyAccessID” button to authenticate using your university credentials.

[![Access ID Login Option](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)

If this is your first time accessing MyAccessID, please ensure that you provide the necessary details and take a moment to review their policy. If you have further issues with using MyAccessID and you have a university email address, please contact your local IT department to ensure you are

ion have even more memory: **10.3 GiB RAM per CPU** for a grand total of 1.15 TiB RAM.
* These nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband.
* These nodes are running [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire Rapids HBM[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids-hbm "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire

* These nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband.
* These nodes are running [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire Rapids HBM[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids-hbm "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire

Hello,
Is there any chance we can add a module for the last version of blast? I think the last one available is 2.11, and it is not complete (for example blastp is missing)
Thanks.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Request / Module Availability
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a newer BLAST module because the currently visible version appears incomplete. The correct initial helpdesk response is to treat this as a software request: check whether a newer supported module already exists and, if not, investigate whether one can be added.

ACTION
1) Check whether a newer supported BLAST module already exists.
2) If not, investigate the request as a software-installation/update request.
3) If a newer module becomes available, send the exact module name and load command to the user.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello,

We can investigate whether a newer BLAST module can be provided.

If we are able to add a newer supported version, we will send you the exact module name and load command.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Module Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting an update to the BLAST module, specifically the latest version which includes `blastp`. The current version available is 2.11, and it is incomplete [User Query].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the current version of BLAST available on the system using `module avail blast` [1][2].
2. Check if a newer version of BLAST is available for installation.
3. If a newer version is available, proceed with the installation and update the module.
4. If a newer version is not available, escalate to the software management team for further action.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details on the specific features or functionalities you need from the latest version of BLAST?
- Are there any specific dependencies or requirements for the new version?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,

Thank you for your request. We will check the availability of the latest version of BLAST and proceed with the update if possible. We will get back to you with more information soon.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any new module installation follows the documented procedures and guidelines [1][2][3][4][5].
- Verify compatibility and dependencies before installing a new module version.

## Using the modules environment[¶](#using-the-modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

### Information on the available modules[¶](#information-on-the-available-modules "Permalink to this headline")

Finding out which modules (and hence which compilers, libraries and
software) are available on the system is performed using the
`module avail` command:

```
[user@system ~]$ module avail
```

This will list all the names and versions of the modules available on
the service. Not all of them may work for your account however, due to,
for example, licencing restrictions. You will notice that for many
modules we have more than one version, each of which is identified by a
version number. One of these versions is the default. As the
service develops the default version will change.

### How to redirect module output[¶](#how-to-redirect-module-output "Permalink to this headline")

If using the BASH shell you may redirect standard error (**stderr**) to standard output (**stdout**) using the `2>&1` syntax. This enables you to search for specific items in the available modules:

```
#The below command will list all available modules with the phrase 'python-3.6' in them
[user@system ~]$ module avail 2>&1 | grep -i python-3.6
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-23fr5u4
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-64u3a4w
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-vag3zpv
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-xk7ym4l
python-3.6.2-gcc-5.4.0-me5fsee
python-3.6.2-intel-17.0.4-lv2lxsb
```

### Identifying currently loaded modules[¶](#identifying-currently-loaded-modules "Permalink to this headline")

The simple `module

n-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-23fr5u4
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-64u3a4w
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-vag3zpv
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-xk7ym4l
python-3.6.2-gcc-5.4.0-me5fsee
python-3.6.2-intel-17.0.4-lv2lxsb
```

### Identifying currently loaded modules[¶](#identifying-currently-loaded-modules "Permalink to this headline")

The simple `module list` command will give the names of the modules
and their versions you have presently loaded in your envionment:

```
[user@login-e-11 ~]$ module list
Currently Loaded Modulefiles:
1) slurm                          6) turbovnc/2.0.1                11) intel/impi/2017.4/intel       16) intel/bundles/complib/2017.4
2) rhel7/global                   7) vgl/2.5.1/64                  12) intel/libs/idb/2017.4         17) rhel7/default-peta4
3) spack/current                  8) singularity/current           13) intel/libs/tbb/2017.4
4) dot                            9) intel/compilers/2017.4        14) intel/libs/ipp/2017.4
5) java/jdk1.8.0_45              10) intel/mkl/2017.4              15) intel/libs/daal/2017.4
```

### Loading, unloading and swapping modules[¶](#loading-unloading-and-swapping-modules "Permalink to this headline")

To load a module to use `module add` or `module load`. For example,
to load the pgi commpiler into the development environment:

```
module load pgi
```

This will load the default version of the pgi commpilers Library. If
you need a specfic version of the module, you can add more information:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

will load version 18.1 for you, regardless of the default. If you
want to clean up, `module remove` will

```
module load pgi/2018
```

will load version 18.1 for you, regardless of the default. If you
want to clean up, `module remove` will remove a loaded module:

```
module remove pgi
```

(or `module rm pgi` or
`module unload pgi`) will unload whatever version of pgi (even if it is not the default) you might have
loaded.

## Private and group modules[¶](#private-and-group-modules "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to create private modules using the following procedure:

1. Create a folder named `privatemodules` in the home directory with:

```
cd ~
mkdir privatemodules
```

2. Add module files in the `privatemodules` folder
3. Access the private modules with:

```
module load use.own
```

This command can be added in the .bashrc file for accessing the private modules without entering the above command everytime the user logins.

3. To list the private modules is done using the `modules av` command. The private modules list will be shown below the other global module lists.

To create group modules a folder with the module files and has to be created in the rds folder that is available to all the members of the group. Moreover, the program files that the modules need must the rds storage too. Both of these folders and files containted must have read permission by all users.

Each user in the group must create a symbolic link to the folder with the group module files. An example of

# AlphaFold[¶](#alphafold "Permalink to this headline")

From the Alphafold website <https://github.com/deepmind/alphafold>

> This package provides an implementation of the inference pipeline of AlphaFold v2.0.
> This is a completely new model that was entered in CASP14 and published in Nature.
> For simplicity, we refer to this model as AlphaFold throughout the rest of this document.
>
> We also provide an implementation of AlphaFold-Multimer. This represents a work in progress and AlphaFold-Multimer isn’t expected to be as stable as our monomer AlphaFold system. Read the guide for how to upgrade and update code.

## AlphaFold data on CSD3[¶](#alphafold-data-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The 2.8TB dataset is stored in:

```
/datasets/public/AlphaFold/data
```

Note that you may need to ls the directory in order for it to be mounted. There are example sequences stored in the input directory.

The dataset has been recently updated (November 2023) so scripts may not work without pointing to new versions of the files. In addition, to address this [issue](https://github.com/google-deepmind/alphafold/issues/810), newer versions of the `uniref` can be found here /datasets/public/AlphaFold/data/uniref30/UniRef30\_2023\_02\*.

There are various ways to run AlphaFold2 on CSD3. We encourage the use of:

To get up and running quickly on CSD3 it is possible to run the Singularity container provided as a module:

```
module load alphafold/2.3.2-singularity
```

 3) singularity/current   7) intel/mkl/2020.2         11) intel/libs/ipp/2020.2   15) use.own
 4) rhel8/global          8) intel/impi/2020.2/intel  12) intel/libs/daal/2020.2  16) bacula/5.2.13
```

The above shows that Slurm (the job queueing system software), as well as the Intel compilers and the Intel MPI environment are loaded (these are actually loaded as a result of loading the default- module, which is loaded automatically on login).

Further commands:

```
module load <module>         -> load module
module unload <module>       -> unload module
module purge                 -> unload all modules
module list                  -> show currently loaded modules
module avail                 -> show available modules
module whatis                -> show available modules with brief explanation
```

There may be a number of historical modules inherited from older systems that appear in response to `module avail`. Please avoid modules with names such as “sandybridge” and “nehalem” in their paths and prefer modules exhibiting the Spack hash (-4qrgkot in the example above).

user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Conda Environment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue is related to the activation of a Conda environment in a Slurm batch job. The user's script correctly loads the miniconda module and sources the .bashrc file, but the Conda environment is not being activated correctly within the job [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify that the `conda init` command has been run to initialize the shell environment for Conda [1][2].
2. Ensure that the `source .bashrc` command is included in the Slurm script to source the .bashrc file [1][2].
3. Check the Slurm job script for any syntax errors or missing commands.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Stefanos,

Thank you for your message. The issue you're experiencing is likely due to the Conda environment not being properly initialized in the Slurm batch job.

Please ensure that you have run the `conda init` command to initialize your shell environment for Conda. This command modifies your .bashrc file and must be sourced in your Slurm script [1][2].

Here is a revised version of your Slurm job script:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake

# Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
module load miniconda

# Source the .bashrc file to initialize Conda
source .bashrc

# Activate the environment
conda activate ml

# Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"

# Alternatively:
echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"

# Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
```

Please try running this script and let us know if you encounter any further issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the `conda init` command has been run to initialize the shell environment for Conda [1][2].
- Always include the `source .bashrc` command in Slurm scripts to source the .bashrc file [1][2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-tools/python.html::chunk::0003
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-tools/python.html::chunk::0002
retrieved_contexts:
scripts[¶](#using-anaconda-python-in-slurm-scripts "Permalink to this headline")

Without running `conda init` the commands `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` will present
the following warning.

```
[user@login-e-15 ~]$ conda activate
CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'.
To initialize your shell, run

    $ conda init <SHELL_NAME>

Currently supported shells are:
- bash
- fish
- tcsh
- xonsh
- zsh
- powershell

See 'conda init --help' for more information and options.

IMPORTANT: You may need to close and restart your shell after running 'conda init'.
```

Since `conda init` injects some logic into your .bashrc file it must be sourced. This is
not an issue for interactive shell sessions as it happens automatically when you login.
However this is not the case for SLURM jobs, therefore it is important to add `source .bashrc`
to your submission script to allow subsequent `conda activate` or `conda deactivate` commands
to work.

### Installing additional Anaconda Python modules[¶](#installing-additional-anaconda-python-modules "Permalink to this headline")

If the central `base` installation does not have a package or module that you require, you
can install this yourself by using *conda environments*.

A conda environemnt is a local copy of the central install that you can then modify
with additional modules/packages or even use different versions of existing
packages.

Full documentation on using conda environments can be found online at [Managing
conda

e and initialize the
sandbox. After that, you just have to activate and deactivate accordingly to
your need.

## Using Anaconda Python[¶](#using-anaconda-python "Permalink to this headline")

To setup your environment to use the Anaconda distributions you should use:

for Python 2:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/2
```

or for Python 3:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
```

You can verify the current version of Python with:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ python --version
Python 3.7.4
```

You can also verify the current version of conda with:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ conda --version
conda 4.7.12
```

If this is the first time using Anaconda Python then it is important to
run the first time `conda init` command to correctly prepare your shell
environemnt for using the full suite of `conda` commands. This only needs
to be done once.

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ which conda
/usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/conda
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ conda init
[... list of modifications not made ...]
modified      /home/user/.bashrc

==> For changes to take effect, close and re-open your current shell. <==
```

Without running `conda init` the commands `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` will present
the following warning.

```
[user@login-e-15 ~]$ conda activate
CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda

fi

fi
# module load cuda if needed

conda create -p openmm-env
conda activate openmm-env/
# Install openmm build matching the CUDA version loaded on the GPU node
conda install -c conda-forge openmm cuda-version=$CUDA_VERSION
```

scripts[¶](#using-anaconda-python-in-slurm-scripts "Permalink to this headline")

Without running `conda init` the commands `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` will present

```
[user@login-e-15 ~]$ conda activate
CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'.
To initialize your shell, run

    $ conda init <SHELL_NAME>

Currently supported shells are:
- bash
- fish
- tcsh
- xonsh
- zsh
- powershell

See 'conda init --help' for more information and options.

IMPORTANT: You may need to close and restart your shell after running 'conda init'.
```

Since `conda init` injects some logic into your .bashrc file it must be sourced. This is
not an issue for interactive shell sessions as it happens automatically when you login.
However this is not the case for SLURM jobs, therefore it is important to add `source .bashrc`
to your submission script to allow subsequent `conda activate` or `conda deactivate` commands
to work.

user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Platform Incident / Filesystem Performance
Technical Action Required: Yes (investigate possible service-side performance issue)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user’s slow Python setup and `import torch` freezes could reflect either a problem in the user environment or a broader shared-filesystem or service issue. Because the slowdown affects multiple environment-creation methods and both home and `hpc-work`, the first response should check for any active service-side problem before focusing only on the user’s setup.

ACTION
1) Check whether there is a broader service issue affecting Python environment creation or shared filesystems.
2) Ask the user to confirm the login node and working path they are using.
3) If no active incident is found, collect the exact commands and timings needed to separate user-environment issues from platform performance issues.
4) Ask the user to retry once those checks have been completed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stelios,

The symptoms you describe may indicate a broader platform performance issue rather than only a mistake in your Python environment setup.

We are checking this on our side. In the meantime, please confirm which login node and working path you are using so that we can investigate further if needed.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Python Environment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing significant delays when setting up and using Python environments on CSD3. The issue persists across different methods (micromamba, virtualenv, miniconda3) and locations (home directory, hpc-work). The problem seems to be related to Python in general, as even basic commands like `python -m venv` are taking an unusually long time to complete.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's account and permissions to ensure there are no restrictions that could be causing the delays.
2. Check the system logs for any anomalies or performance issues that might be affecting Python operations.
3. Consult with the system administrators to see if there are any known issues with the Python modules or the file system.
4. If the issue persists, escalate to a higher-level support team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact commands and steps you followed when setting up the Python environments?
- Have you noticed any error messages or warnings during the setup process?
- Are there any other users experiencing similar issues on CSD3?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Stelios,

Thank you for reaching out. We understand that you are experiencing significant delays when setting up and using Python environments on CSD3. We will investigate this issue further to determine the cause of the delays.

In the meantime, please provide the following information to help us troubleshoot the issue:
1. The exact commands and steps you followed when setting up the Python environments.
2. Any error messages or warnings you encountered during the setup process.
3. Whether other users are experiencing similar issues on CSD3.

We will keep you updated on our progress and let you know as soon as we have more information.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any changes made to the user's environment are documented and communicated clearly to avoid confusion.
- Follow the documented procedures for escalating issues to higher-level support teams when necessary [5].

REFERENCE KEY
[5] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/getting_support.html::chunk::0000

# PyTorch[¶](#pytorch "Permalink to this headline")

The PyTorch [website](https://pytorch.org) describes the library as:

> Tensors and Dynamic neural networks in Python with strong GPU acceleration.

## Setup PyTorch on CSD3[¶](#setup-pytorch-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The python installation will depend on the target partition (CPU or GPU).
To run Pytorch on a CPU partition (cclake, icelake), please do the following:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl # recommended for all RHEL8 CPU partitions (cclake, icelake, sapphire)
module load python/3.11.9/gcc/nptrdpll

# create and activate a virtual environment:

python -m venv ./pytorch-cclake-env
source ./pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# install pytorch into your virtual env (instructions from Intel: https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/installation?platform=cpu&version=v2.7.0%2Bcpu&os=linux%2Fwsl2&package=pip)
python -m pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
python -m pip install intel-extension-for-pytorch
python -m pip install oneccl_bind_pt --extra-index-url https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/release-whl/stable/cpu/us/
```

To run Pytorch on a GPU partition (ampere), please follow the following instructions:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

# create and activate a virtual environment:
python -m

torch on a GPU partition (ampere), please follow the following instructions:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

# create and activate a virtual environment:
python -m venv ./pytorch-ampere-env
source ./pytorch-ampere-env/bin/activate

# install pytorch into your virtual env:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio
```

## Running a PyTorch program[¶](#running-a-pytorch-program "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch will be able to run on both CPU and GPU.

CPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-CPU
#SBATCH --partition cclake
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node 1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task 8
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl

# Activate relevant python virtual environment.
source pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# Let PyTorch use all available CPU cores for OpenMP, MKL and MKL-DNN backends
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK

# Edit and replace 'your_script.py' with your own script
python your_script.py
```

GPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-GPU
#SBATCH --partition ampere
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#! change to gpu:4 to use all 4 GPU cards on a GPU node.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment

# Using Python[¶](#using-python "Permalink to this headline")

CSD3 provides central installations of both Python 2 and Python 3. The central
installation provides some of the most common packages used for scientific
computation and data analysis and additional packages can be added by users by
using either virtual environments or conda environments.

Recent versions of the Python interpreter can be accessed as python/2.7,
python/3.5, python/3.6, python/3.7 or python/3.8. The default is currently
python/3.6. These packages will be automatically upgraded to the latest point
release.

## Using Virtual Environments[¶](#using-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

Virtualenv provides a method for installing new or upgraded Python packages as
a user without the need to ask support to make changes centrally. It is
currently supported in all the Python modules.

### Step-by-step guide[¶](#step-by-step-guide "Permalink to this headline")

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

In short, you can create a sandboxed version, in the directory of your choice
(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

Then activate this

node (see Interactive jobs via the scheduler for starting one).

```
# 1. Load appropriate modules and environment
module purge
module load rhel8/slurm rhel8/global
module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6
module load cuda/12.1 cudnn/8.9_cuda-12.1
# Access to optional ampere modules (uncomment if needed)
#module use /usr/local/software/spack/spack-modules/a100-20210927/linux-centos8-zen2
#module use /usr/local/software/spack/spack-modules/a100-20210927/linux-centos8-zen3

# 2. Install latest Tensorflow in a python virtualenv
python -m venv tensorflow-ampere-env
source ./tensorflow-ampere-env/bin/activate
python -m pip install tensorflow
export CUDNN_PATH=/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/cudnn/8.9_cuda-12.1/lib64
export XLA_FLAGS=--xla_gpu_cuda_data_dir=$CUDA_PATH
# 3. Test your installation (be in a interative session
# via sintr or submit a job to a gpu compute node)
cat << 'EOF' > helloworld.py
#!/usr/bin/env python
import tensorflow as tf
mnist = tf.keras.datasets.mnist

(x_train, y_train),(x_test, y_test) = mnist.load_data()
x_train, x_test = x_train / 255.0, x_test / 255.0

model = tf.keras.models.Sequential([
          tf.keras.layers.Flatten(input_shape=(28, 28)),
          tf.keras.layers.Dense(512, activation=tf.nn.relu),
          tf.keras.layers.Dropout(0.2),
          tf.keras.layers.Dense(10, activation=tf.nn.softmax)
          ])
model.compile(optimizer='adam',
          loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy',
          metrics=['accuracy'])

model.fit(x_train, y_train, epochs=5)
model.evaluate(x_test, y_test)
EOF
```

## Running Tensorflow[¶](#running-tensorflow

# Getting Support on CSD3[¶](#getting-support-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Please consult the following advice:

* **Understand the limitations:**

#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

# create and activate a virtual environment:
python -m venv ./pytorch-ampere-env
source ./pytorch-ampere-env/bin/activate

# install pytorch into your virtual env:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio
```

## Running a PyTorch program[¶](#running-a-pytorch-program "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch will be able to run on both CPU and GPU.

CPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-CPU
#SBATCH --partition cclake
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node 1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task 8
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl

# Activate relevant python virtual environment.
source pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# Let PyTorch use all available CPU cores for OpenMP, MKL and MKL-DNN backends
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK

# Edit and replace 'your_script.py' with your own script
python your_script.py
```

GPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-GPU
#SBATCH --partition ampere
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#! change to gpu:4 to use all 4 GPU cards on a GPU node.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment

# Using Python[¶](#using-python "Permalink to this headline")

CSD3 provides central installations of both Python 2 and Python 3. The central
installation provides some of the most common packages used for scientific
computation and data analysis and additional packages can be added by users by
using either virtual environments or conda environments.

Recent versions of the Python interpreter can be accessed as python/2.7,
python/3.5, python/3.6, python/3.7 or python/3.8. The default is currently
python/3.6. These packages will be automatically upgraded to the latest point
release.

## Using Virtual Environments[¶](#using-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

Virtualenv provides a method for installing new or upgraded Python packages as
a user without the need to ask support to make changes centrally. It is
currently supported in all the Python modules.

### Step-by-step guide[¶](#step-by-step-guide "Permalink to this headline")

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

In short, you can create a sandboxed version, in the directory of your choice
(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

Then activate this

node (see Interactive jobs via the scheduler for starting one).

```
# 1. Load appropriate modules and environment
module purge
module load rhel8/slurm rhel8/global
module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6
module load cuda/12.1 cudnn/8.9_cuda-12.1
# Access to optional ampere modules (uncomment if needed)
#module use /usr/local/software/spack/spack-modules/a100-20210927/linux-centos8-zen2
#module use /usr/local/software/spack/spack-modules/a100-20210927/linux-centos8-zen3

# 2. Install latest Tensorflow in a python virtualenv
python -m venv tensorflow-ampere-env
source ./tensorflow-ampere-env/bin/activate
python -m pip install tensorflow
export CUDNN_PATH=/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/cudnn/8.9_cuda-12.1/lib64
export XLA_FLAGS=--xla_gpu_cuda_data_dir=$CUDA_PATH
# 3. Test your installation (be in a interative session
# via sintr or submit a job to a gpu compute node)
cat << 'EOF' > helloworld.py
#!/usr/bin/env python
import tensorflow as tf
mnist = tf.keras.datasets.mnist

(x_train, y_train),(x_test, y_test) = mnist.load_data()
x_train, x_test = x_train / 255.0, x_test / 255.0

model = tf.keras.models.Sequential([
          tf.keras.layers.Flatten(input_shape=(28, 28)),
          tf.keras.layers.Dense(512, activation=tf.nn.relu),
          tf.keras.layers.Dropout(0.2),
          tf.keras.layers.Dense(10, activation=tf.nn.softmax)
          ])
model.compile(optimizer='adam',
          loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy',
          metrics=['accuracy'])

model.fit(x_train, y_train, epochs=5)
model.evaluate(x_test, y_test)
EOF
```

## Running Tensorflow[¶](#running-tensorflow

# Getting Support on CSD3[¶](#getting-support-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Please consult the following advice:

* **Understand the limitations:**

user_input:
Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

Best regards,
HPC Support

```
The Singularity image was built from Deepmind’s docker script and has been tested on the A100 nodes. The MSA construction and model inference are done on the same node type - it isn’t easy to decouple the two steps without using separate implementations (see below). Users can choose to run on CPU but the inference step takes considerably longer than on a GPU. Running on a GPU means that the CPU preprocessing (MSA) step can dominate the running time (depending on the particular sequence we wish to predict the structure of).

## Current Issues[¶](#current-issues "Permalink to this headline")

We are aware of the slow preprocessing time of hhblits on CSD3 and are working to improve this. For the small database it’s possible to pre-stage the data on the local ssd drive (with rsync), but this is not possible for the full database as it exceeds the capacity of the local ssd drive.
```

## Running Tensorflow[¶](#running-tensorflow

ion have even more memory: **10.3 GiB RAM per CPU** for a grand total of 1.15 TiB RAM.
* These nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband.
* These nodes are running [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire Rapids HBM[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids-hbm "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire

Each directory has a hidden `.snapshot` subdirectory containing previous versions of data. Under these it is possible to search and browse for a good version of the file (which can be copied back to your home directory). For example,

```
/home/js123/workfiles/.snapshot/hourly_2022-07-06_18\:00/
```

contains an image of the /home/js123/workfiles directory as it was at 18:00 on 6th July 2022.

## Interconnect[¶](#interconnect "Permalink to this headline")

The CSD3 components are shown in the simplified topology diagram.
Only a few of the racks within the system, however it should be noted from a networking point of view each rack is composed of two 32 node leaves. Within this nodes are non blocking. To speed between racks the network is 2:1 in oversubscription. The GPU system differs in interconnect and uses Infiniband EDR. This is similarly 2:1 in its oversubscription, with 18 nodes per leaf switch. The GPU system accesses the Lustre storage via I/O nodes using LNET. These LNET routers do not show any performance reduction in benchmarks run both direct from the OPA attached compute nodes and the GPU system.

![../_images/OPA-Topo-sim.png](../_images/OPA-Topo-sim.png)

## File IO Performance Guidelines[¶](#file-io-performance-guidelines "Permalink to this headline")

RDS is build from 5 pools of 1PB Lustre. This helps when running the system for stability and flexibility.
Each Lustre is made up of 24 OSTs. Object Storage Targets are the backend storage that your data is stored upon.

To make best use of this resource:

* Your home directory should not be used for I/O from

Dear support team,
I am trying to log in to my RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) using FileZilla. I am following the instructions given in "Reseach Storage Documentation”. Unfortunately, I am denied access after entering my password and the TOTP verification code (see screenshot).
I can succesfully log in to [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/), so I assume I have the correct password. I have also succesfully set up MFA/TOTP for CSD3 (see screenshot), and I am correctly entering the code when prompted to do so.
Maybe I need a different password that is specific for this service?
Kind regards
Christina
Prof. Dr. med. Christina Zielinski
Professor of Infection and Immunity
Department of Pathology
University of Cambridge
Tennis Court Road
Cambridge CB2 1QP
cez22@cam.ac.uk

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RCS Access / Group Membership / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The access failure is unlikely to be explained by a separate service-specific password, especially since the user can log in elsewhere successfully. The most likely next steps are to check whether there is an access-control/group-membership problem and to confirm that the user has generated the correct MFA token for RCS/CSD3 access via `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

ACTION
1) Check whether the account has the correct storage/access-group membership.
2) Instruct the user to create the correct TOTP via:
   `ssh <crsid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
3) Share the MFA documentation.
4) Remind the user of the normal login path and storage path once access has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Christina,

You should not need a separate password for this service. We will check your storage access permissions on our side.

You will also need to create the MFA token that works with RCS/CSD3 access. Please run:
`ssh cez22@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the on-screen instructions. Guidance is here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

Best wishes,
HPC Support

Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues logging into RCS storage using FileZilla despite successfully logging into the web interface and setting up MFA/TOTP. The issue might be related to the TOTP configuration or the specifics of FileZilla usage.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's TOTP setup and ensure it is correctly configured for CSD3 [1][2].
2. Check if the user is using the correct RCS storage path and credentials.
3. Advise the user to use the RDS gateway (rds.uis.cam.ac.uk) for file transfers as it requests TOTP less frequently [1][3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you have followed the exact steps in the "Research Storage Documentation" for setting up MFA/TOTP?
- Have you tried using the RDS gateway (rds.uis.cam.ac.uk) for file transfers instead of FileZilla?
- Please provide the exact error message you receive when attempting to log in via FileZilla.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Christina,

Thank you for your message. It appears that the issue might be related to the TOTP configuration or the specifics of FileZilla usage.

Please try the following steps:
1. Ensure that your TOTP setup is correctly configured for CSD3. You can refer to the MFA guidance documentation for detailed instructions [1][2].
2. Consider using the RDS gateway (rds.uis.cam.ac.uk) for file transfers as it requests TOTP less frequently [1][3].

If the issue persists, please provide the exact error message you receive when attempting to log in via FileZilla.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that TOTP is correctly configured before attempting to log in via FileZilla.
- Prefer using the RDS gateway for file transfers to minimize TOTP requests [1][3].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html::chunk::0010
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html::chunk::0009
[3] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html::chunk::0011

* I am unable to login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and now I get “Connection refused”.

  > + Make sure you are using the same userid, password or public key as you would to login.hpc.cam.ac.uk. As with the login nodes, failing to authenticate three times in succession to multi will cause your address to be blocked for 20 minutes, so if this happens please wait this long before trying again.
* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

  > + If you still have the QR code and secret key on your screen then delete the account in your Authenticator and recreate it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*). Otherwise please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again, and select account type **Other**.
* When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.

  > + Setting **export LANG=en\_GB.UTF-8** in Mac terminals before performing the ssh may stop this happening - however if this has already happened you will need to contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again.
* Connection sharing doesn’t work with Filezilla and I keep seeing TOTP requests during file transfers.

## How do I reset my TOTP for SSH to CSD3?[¶](#how-do-i-reset-my-totp-for-ssh-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

If you need to reset your TOTP second factor, e.g. because you no longer have access to your original phone, please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk.
The TOTP second factor is an important protection against user account compromise, and an identity check is therefore needed when a request to replace it is received. This is similar to the existing process for resetting University passwords in a context where face to face identity checks are not feasible most of the time.

When contacting the service desk to request a reset of MFA, please be sure to send the email from the email address we have registered against your user account.

The service desk agent will arrange a time at which to hold a brief video call with you at which you will be asked to present photographic proof of identity in the form of one of the following:

> * Passport
> * Driving licence
> * University card (for members of the University of Cambridge).

Expired forms of the above are acceptable as proof of identity. Following completion of this check, the TOTP secret will be reset and you will be able to revisit multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk to repeat the setup procedure.

* I don’t have a smart phone on which I can install an OATH-compliant TOTP app such as Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy or FreeOTP.

  > + The proper solution for you is to ask your department to purchase an inexpensive OATH TOTP compatible token device.

  > + Any device needs to support **6 digit, SHA1** OATH TOTP with **30 seconds time interval**, which non-programmable devices may well not do. In particular, please note that the *SafeID/Classic* model provided by the UIS for Microsoft services **is known not to work** with the Google style of TOTP used on CSD3.

access the special MFA node:

```
ssh <username>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

The first time you connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk (as for any new destination with SSH) you will see a message similar to this:

```
The authenticity of host 'multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk (128.232.222.63)' can't be established.
ED25519 key fingerprint is SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so.
```

Please check that the fingerprint listed matches one of those listed as current at <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html>.

You may also see additional lines like the following:

```
This host key is known by the following other names/addresses:
    ~/.ssh/known_hosts:18: login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
    (4 additional names omitted)
```

This is to be expected as all login nodes on CSD3 share a single set of host keys. Provided the key fingerprint is current it is ok to respond **yes** to the final question:

```
Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])?
```

**University of Cambridge users**: You will need to enter your University password.

**External users**: You will need to use your SSH key or your password, depending on how your account was created.

If TOTP is already configured for your account, a message will be printed and the connection closed:

# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a
